title: I Saved a Fly the Other Day
author: Kevin Martin - 15.08
content:
I Saved a Fly the Other Day

I saved a fly the other day.
It was lying on its back
In a shallow pool of water
On a crumpled plastic sack.

It didn’t move or cry for help,
Just lay there, slick and still.
I sat smoking fags and drinking tea
With time enough to kill.

Then I saw a leg move.
Then I saw another.
It wasn’t dead at all —
Just stuck there in the water.

With a folded scrap of paper
I set it on its feet.
It trembled for a moment
Then lifted in the heat.

It didn’t say a thank you,
Or wave a cheery wing.
It simply flew off out of sight
As if I hadn’t done a thing.

Now I eat meat and drive a car,
I warm the air each day.
But I am human — so I tell myself
It doesn’t count that way.

That fly is buzzing somewhere now,
A nuisance, most would say.
But it won’t bring the oceans up
To wash our towns away.

So next time you see a fly
And it’s driving you quite mad,
Just take a second, have a look,
And don’t swat it quite so fast.

Cos when we’re gone
And it’s all made good,
We won’t be coming back —
Just tiny flies like him.

title: The Midnight Hour
author: Kevin Martin - 18.36
content:
A phone box. Night.

The rain has stopped.
Light stains the pavement with dull, bright colours.
Traffic hisses past without slowing.

A man.

A woman.

They stand close, but not quite touching.
They are waiting.

Across the street, an upstairs window glows with too much light. A woman moves behind the glass — bare but for stocking, the tops dark against her legs. She dances slowly for someone inside the room, perhaps only for herself.

There would be music.

It is unheard.

A scene sealed off from the night.

They don’t comment.
They don’t look away either.

He shifts first. Checks his watch. Then a small step.
His hand comes to the back of her skirt, firm, necessary — guiding, protective.

Warm.

She lets herself be moved. The skirt sways as she settles where he puts her.
Close to him.

He pauses to watch the windows again, as the movement quickens, then stills.

He draws her closer. Not rushed. Not gentle.
He holds her.

Pulling.

She rises onto her toes.
Balanced.
Suspended.

Her face is suddenly near — closer than it should be — breath shared, the faint sweet odour of make-up, cheap perfume cut with cold air.

Her coat is opened.
The soft crackle of fabric.
Loud in the quiet.

This is the part he remembers.
Always this part.

Her breast leans into his hand.

Not deliberate.
Not accidental either.
Just weight finding support.

His hand tightens.
Doesn’t move.

For a second his body remembers before he does.

The closeness.
The promise.
The instant before it changes forever.

He holds her there.

Unmoving.

The phone rings.

There is a pause.
Silent regret.

The phone box door is heavy after her lightness.
The sound loud as it closes behind him.

“Yes.”

He watches the girl through the glass. She is watching the window.

“Yes, we are here.”

He listens.

“Yes. An hour from now.”

He taps the glass and nods at her.

“Yes, I will call after it is done.”

He closes his eyes.

“Yes, as agreed.”

He listens again.

“Yes, I will make sure.”

He puts the phone down and opens the door.

She looks at him.

“Yes.”

It is all he needs to say.

*

A country house. Before dawn.


Rich people live here.

It is dark and still, holding its breath.

He drives slowly with the lights off. Stops and waits.

Nothing. No sound. No movement.

He looks across at the girl.

There is a quarter moon.

The gravel crunches softly under their feet.

He kneels at the French windows. Wooden. A simple lock, opened with a thin blade. She stands close, her leg against his arm as he works.

The window clicks open.

He puts his hand on her leg and feels the warmth.

As he stands his hand moves up, raising the skirt.

At the top of the stocking, a moment of cool skin.

Now he can smell her.

Again they are close.

He grips her there and pushes her inside.

It is dark and they stand still until they can see.

They move quickly now. No noise. They know where to go.

A hall.

Stairs.

A corridor.

The door and into a bedroom.

Moonlight filters through lace curtains.

He turns to face her as he takes the heavy gun from his pocket. The suppressor is fitted.

The man hands it to the woman.

In the bed is a figure sleeping.

The woman walks forward.

There is a compressed sound, then another.

She turns and hands back the gun.

He grabs her by the hair and kisses her.

She presses against him.

Then they leave.
